2605.06191.title: Systematic Evaluation of Large Language Models for Post-Discharge Clinical Action Extraction
2605.06191.paper_summary: The work in this paper evaluates zero-shot and few-shot large language models (LLMs) for safety-critical clinical action extraction using the CLIP discharge-note dataset, with particular emphasis on transitions of care and post-discharge patient safety. To manage the complexity of clinical documentation, we introduce a two-stage extraction framework that decomposes discharge notes, that are written in narrative form, into fine-grained, explicitly actionable clinical tasks through a staged prompting strategy. Our contributions include a systematic assessment of generative LLMs for clinical action extraction, a detailed comparison between general-purpose LLMs and task-specific supervised BERT-based models, and an analysis of annotation inconsistencies across different action categories. We show that contemporary LLMs achieve performance comparable to or exceeding supervised models on binary actionability detection, while supervised baselines retain a meaningful advantage on fine-grained multi-label category classification, despite the absence of task-specific fine-tuning and under strict data-privacy constraints. Qualitative error analysis reveals that many failures stem from misalignment between model reasoning and dataset annotation conventions, particularly in cases involving implicit clinical actions and rigid structural labeling rules. These results indicate that reported performance reflects model limitations due to lack of clinical reasoning, that is not captured by plain annotations. Labels without rationales make it impossible to distinguish clinical reasoning failures from annotation convention mismatches. Advancing clinical NLP requires reasoning-annotated datasets that document why specific spans are actionable, not merely which spans were labeled, enabling proper evaluation of model clinical understanding.
2605.06191.paper_summary_zh: 這篇論文的工作評估了零樣本和少樣本的大型語言模型（LLMs）在安全關鍵的臨床行動提取中的應用，使用了 CLIP 出院記錄數據集，特別強調護理過渡和出院後患者安全。為了管理臨床文檔的複雜性，我們提出了一個兩階段的提取框架，通過分階提示策略將以敘述形式撰寫的出院記錄分解為細粒度、明確可行的臨床任務。我們的貢獻包括對生成性 LLMs 在臨床行動提取中的系統評估、通用 LLMs 與任務特定的監督式 BERT 模型之間的詳細比較，以及對不同行動類別之間標註不一致性的分析。我們顯示當代 LLMs 在二元可行性檢測方面的表現可與監督模型相媲美或超越，而監督基準在細粒度多標籤類別分類上仍保持著顯著的優勢，儘管缺乏任務特定的微調並受到嚴格的數據隱私限制。定性錯誤分析顯示，許多失敗源於模型推理與數據集標註慣例之間的不一致，特別是在涉及隱性臨床行動和嚴格結構標籤規則的情況下。這些結果表明，報告的性能反映了模型因缺乏臨床推理而產生的限制，而這些限制並未通過簡單的標註捕捉到。缺乏理據的標籤使得無法區分臨床推理失敗與標註慣例不匹配。推進臨床自然語言處理需要標註推理的數據集，記錄為何特定範圍是可行的，而不僅僅是哪些範圍被標註，從而使模型的臨床理解能夠得到適當的評估。
2605.06191.author: Shivali Dalmia et.al.
2605.06191.authors: Shivali Dalmia, Ananya Mantravadi, Prasanna Desikan
